intro.overview: This week continues your security training at NEXUS with a deep dive into authentication systems. You’ll master SSH key generation, understand why key permissions matter, configure SSH for secure connections, and explore multi-factor authentication concepts. These skills are essential for the Gulf Coast Healthcare engagement next week — their infrastructure relies on certificate-based authentication.

mentorQuote.title: Blue Team Lead, NEXUS Security
mentorQuote.quote: Attackers have to be right once. Defenders have to be right every time. That’s why we layer defenses — and authentication is your first layer. Passwords are the weakest link. This week, you’ll learn what replaces them.

focusAreas.title: SSH Keypair Generation
focusAreas.description: Generating RSA and ED25519 keypairs — comparing the two algorithms and understanding when each is appropriate in modern infrastructure.

focusAreas.title: File Permission Security
focusAreas.description: Linux permission octal notation (600, 644, 700) and why SSH refuses to use keys with incorrect permissions — a live demonstration of least privilege.

focusAreas.title: authorized_keys and SSH Config
focusAreas.description: Setting up server-side key authorization and client-side config aliases — how professional analysts manage connections to dozens of servers efficiently.

focusAreas.title: Multi-Factor Authentication
focusAreas.description: Configuring SSH to require both a private key and a password — implementing real two-factor authentication with factors from different categories.

focusAreas.title: Defense in Depth
focusAreas.description: Directory permissions (700) combined with file permissions (600) provide two independent layers of access control — neither layer alone is sufficient.

focusAreas.title: Authentication Factor Categories
focusAreas.description: Something you know, something you have, something you are — why a password and a security question are NOT two factors, but a key and a password are.

objectives: Generate SSH keypairs using both RSA and ED25519 algorithms
Configure proper file permissions for SSH keys
Set up authorized_keys and SSH config files

callouts.title: Prerequisite
callouts.text: This lab requires a working Kali Linux VM from Lab 1.1 and completed work from Lab 2.1: Cryptographic Foundations. Both must be done before starting here.

callouts.title: Save For Lab 2.3
callouts.text: This is Week 2 of a 3-week module. Add this week’s screenshots to your existing Module2_Screenshots folder. You should have 9 screenshots from Lab 2.1. This week adds 8 more.

timeGuide.timesheetNote: This is Week 2 of a 3-week module. Time here is still internal NEXUS training — not yet billable to Gulf Coast Healthcare. Add this week's 8 screenshots to your existing Module2_Screenshots folder. You should now have 17 total (9 from Lab 2.1 plus 8 from this lab). You will use all of them in your Lab 2.3 Engagement Packet.

content.whatYoureDoing: Generating SSH keypairs using both RSA and ED25519 algorithms. You’ll compare the two key types and understand when to use each.
content.whyItMatters: SSH key authentication is the industry standard for secure remote access. Unlike passwords, keys are cryptographically strong, can’t be guessed through brute force, and provide non-repudiation. Every server you’ll manage in your career will use key-based authentication.

content.title: Blue Team Lead, NEXUS Security
content.quote: ED25519 is the modern choice — smaller keys, faster operations, same security. But you’ll still see RSA everywhere. Know both.

content.part: Part A: Generate an RSA keypair

steps.text: Create a working directory for this lab:
Change into your working directory:
Generate a 4096-bit RSA key (press Enter for defaults, use passphrase nexus2026):

whatThisDoes.concept: ssh-keygen generates SSH keypairs for passwordless authentication. Like the RSA keys from Lab 2.1, SSH keys use asymmetric cryptography — but formatted for the SSH protocol. The private key stays on your machine; the public key goes on any server you want to access.
whatThisDoes.output: You’ll see a randomart image — a visual fingerprint of your key. Two files are created: id_rsa_lab (private, keep secret) and id_rsa_lab.pub (public, safe to share).

flags.description: Key type: RSA (the classic, widely compatible algorithm)
Key size: 4096 bits (double the NIST minimum for stronger security)
Output file path (avoids overwriting your real SSH keys)
Comment embedded in the key — helps identify which key is which on servers with many authorized keys

steps.text: List the generated files:
View the public key contents:

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the RSA key generation showing the randomart and file listing. Save as 10-keygen-rsa.png

content.part: Part B: Generate an ED25519 keypair

steps.text: Generate an ED25519 key (modern, faster, smaller):

whatThisDoes.concept: ED25519 is a modern elliptic curve algorithm that provides equivalent security to RSA-4096 with much smaller keys and faster operations. Most security professionals now recommend ED25519 for new deployments — shorter keys, faster math, no known weaknesses. You’ll still encounter RSA on legacy systems, which is why you learned both.

steps.text: List all key files:
Compare the key sizes:

whatThisDoes.output: wc -c counts bytes in each file. The RSA public key will be roughly 750 bytes while ED25519 is about 100 bytes — yet they provide comparable security. Smaller is better when security is equal.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the ED25519 generation and size comparison. Notice the ED25519 public key is about 100 bytes while RSA is about 750 bytes. Save as 11-keygen-ed25519.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Weak passphrase: Empty passphrases leave keys unprotected if stolen
Overwriting existing keys: Check for existing keys before generating new ones
Wrong key type: Some older systems don’t support ED25519 — know your target

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Two screenshots showing both RSA and ED25519 key generation. The ED25519 public key is about 100 bytes while RSA is about 750 bytes — same security, smaller footprint.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 6 covers asymmetric cryptography fundamentals. Chapter 11 explains how public key authentication replaces passwords — the keys you generate here implement “something you have” authentication.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: Why is ED25519 considered more secure than RSA of equivalent key length? Research the mathematical differences between elliptic curve and integer factorization cryptography.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: OpenSSH Manual — ssh-keygen. Available at: https://man.openbsd.org/ssh-keygen

content.whatYoureDoing: Understanding and configuring proper file permissions for SSH keys. You’ll see why SSH refuses to use keys with incorrect permissions and how to fix common permission problems.
content.whyItMatters: A private key with world-readable permissions is a security disaster. SSH clients will refuse to use improperly secured keys, and attackers specifically look for misconfigured key files. Proper permissions are the difference between security and breach.

steps.text: Check the current permissions on your keys:

whatThisDoes.concept: In Linux, every file has three permission groups: Owner, Group, and Others. Each group gets three bits: r (read=4), w (write=2), x (execute=1). Add them up per group to get octal notation. Private keys should be 600 (-rw-------), public keys 644 (-rw-r--r--), and the ~/.ssh directory 700 (drwx------).
whatThisDoes.output: You should see -rw------- for private keys and -rw-r--r-- for public keys. The first column is the permission string.

steps.text: View the same permissions in octal notation:

whatThisDoes.output: Private keys should show 600 and public keys 644 — confirming what ls -la showed. These are the correct, SSH-enforced permission levels.

content.part: Part B: Demonstrate the risk of weak permissions

steps.text: Copy the private key to a shared directory for this demonstration:

whatThisDoes.concept: Your home directory (/home/kali) has permissions 700 — other users can’t enter it. /tmp is world-readable and world-writable, which is realistic: in enterprise environments, files often end up in shared directories like /tmp or /var/www. In those locations, file permissions are your only line of defense.

steps.text: Make the copy world-readable (INSECURE — for demonstration only):

whatThisDoes.concept: You’re changing this key from 600 (owner read/write only) to 644 (owner read/write, group can read, others can read). The permissions change from -rw------- to -rw-r--r--. That r-- at the end means every user on this machine can now read this file — fine for public keys, a disaster for private keys.

steps.text: Prove it — read the key as a completely different user:

whatThisDoes.concept: sudo -u nobody runs the following command as the nobody account — a minimal, unprivileged account with no special access to your files. Think of it as a stand-in for any random user or compromised process on this machine.
whatThisDoes.output: You should see the entire private key printed to your terminal, starting with -----BEGIN OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY-----. This is the actual cryptographic secret. Anyone who has this data can impersonate you on any system where this key is authorized.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the output showing the nobody user successfully reading your private key. The -----BEGIN OPENSSH PRIVATE KEY----- header should be visible. Save as 12-permission-error.png

content.part: Part C: Fix the permissions and verify

steps.text: Restrict the file back to owner-only:

whatThisDoes.concept: chmod 600 sets permissions to -rw-------: 6 = read+write for owner, 0 = nothing for group, 0 = nothing for others. The change takes effect immediately — the kernel enforces it on the very next access attempt.

steps.text: Try reading the key as nobody again:

whatThisDoes.output: You should now see: cat: /tmp/demo_key: Permission denied. Same command, same user, same file — only three digits changed (644 → 600). This is the principle of least privilege in action.

steps.text: Clean up the demo file:

whatThisDoes.concept: Good security hygiene: don’t leave copies of sensitive material in shared directories, even in a lab.

steps.text: Confirm your original keys still have correct permissions:

whatThisDoes.output: Private keys should show 600 and public keys 644. The demonstration only affected the copy in /tmp, not your originals.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the Permission denied output from the nobody user, along with the stat output confirming your original keys are still secure. Save as 13-permission-fixed.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Leaving copies in /tmp: Always clean up after demonstrations
Wrong directory permissions: The ~/.ssh directory itself must be 700 (drwx------)
Public vs private: Public keys are designed to be shared (644). Private keys must always be 600
Defense in depth: Set both directory and file permissions correctly — don’t rely on a single layer

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Two screenshots demonstrating the complete break/fix cycle. Screenshot 12 shows the vulnerability (another user reading your private key). Screenshot 13 shows the fix (Permission denied after restoring 600), plus stat confirmation your originals are secure.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 11 covers access control and the principle of least privilege. What you just demonstrated is the foundation of all access control on Linux systems — the kernel checks file permissions on every read, write, and execute operation.

content.whatYoureDoing: Setting up SSH infrastructure: the authorized_keys file that controls who can connect, the SSH config file that simplifies connections, and testing key-based authentication to localhost.
content.whyItMatters: Understanding SSH configuration is essential for any security professional. The authorized_keys file is your access control list — every public key there can authenticate. The config file lets you manage connections to dozens of servers efficiently. Gulf Coast uses this exact setup for their infrastructure.

content.title: Blue Team Lead, NEXUS Security
content.quote: Never rely on a single control. Layer your defenses so one failure doesn’t mean total compromise. SSH config files let you enforce security policies across all your connections.

steps.text: Create the .ssh directory if it doesn’t exist:
Set secure directory permissions:
Add your public key to authorized_keys:

whatThisDoes.concept: The authorized_keys file is an access control list — every public key listed there can authenticate as this user. The >> operator appends rather than overwrites, preserving existing keys. This is how SSH key-based auth works: your client offers a private key, the server checks if the matching public key is in authorized_keys, and if so, grants access without a password.

steps.text: Set secure file permissions:
Verify the directory contents:
Confirm the authorized key:

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the .ssh directory listing and authorized_keys content. Save as 14-authorized-keys.png

steps.text: Start the SSH service if not running:
Verify SSH is running:
Connect to localhost using your key:

whatThisDoes.concept: Adding a command after the hostname (whoami) tells SSH to run that single command on the remote system and return the output, instead of opening an interactive shell. This is how automated scripts and CI/CD pipelines use SSH.

flags.description: Disables password fallback so the connection only succeeds with the key
Specifies which private key to use (identity file)

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the successful SSH connection using key-based authentication. Save as 15-ssh-keyauth.png

content.part: Part C: Create an SSH config file

steps.text: Open a new config file in nano:

whatThisDoes.concept: nano is a simple terminal-based text editor. The commands at the bottom use ^ to mean the Ctrl key — so ^O means Ctrl+O. Since the file doesn’t exist yet, nano will create it when you save.

steps.text: Type or paste the following contents exactly (use spaces, not tabs for indentation):
steps.command:
Host labserver
    HostName localhost
    User kali
    IdentityFile ~/lab2-auth/id_ed25519_lab
    IdentitiesOnly yes

whatThisDoes.concept: Host labserver — the alias you’ll type. HostName localhost — the actual server address. User kali — the username. IdentityFile — which private key to use. IdentitiesOnly yes — only try the specified key, preventing SSH from cycling through all keys in ~/.ssh/.

steps.text: Save and exit nano: press <kbd>Ctrl+O</kbd>, then <kbd>Enter</kbd> to confirm the filename, then <kbd>Ctrl+X</kbd> to exit.
Lock down config file permissions:

whatThisDoes.concept: Your SSH config contains key paths and server details. Setting 600 ensures only you can read or modify it — the same protection level as your private keys.

steps.text: Verify the config file contents:
Test the connection using your alias:

whatThisDoes.output: Instead of typing the full command from Part B, you just typed ssh labserver whoami. The SSH config resolved labserver to all the connection details automatically. You should see your username with no password prompt.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture the SSH config file contents and successful connection using the alias. Save as 16-ssh-config.png

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Wrong key in authorized_keys: Make sure you’re adding the .pub file, not the private key
Line breaks in key: The public key must be all on one line
Config indentation: Use spaces, not tabs; indent options under Host

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: Three screenshots showing authorized_keys setup, successful key-based SSH connection, and SSH config file usage. You should be able to connect without any password prompt.

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 11 covers remote access security and authentication configuration. The authorized_keys mechanism implements trust relationships — only approved public keys can authenticate.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: What are common authentication vulnerabilities? Research credential stuffing, brute force attacks, and session hijacking.

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: OWASP Authentication Cheat Sheet. Available at: https://cheatsheetseries.owasp.org/cheatsheets/Authentication_Cheat_Sheet.html

content.whatYoureDoing: Configuring your SSH server to require multi-factor authentication — both your private key and your account password before granting access. Right now, your server accepts key-only login. By the end of this checkpoint, a stolen key alone won’t be enough to get in.
content.whyItMatters: Single-factor authentication is the root cause of countless breaches. MFA isn’t optional anymore — it’s required by HIPAA, PCI-DSS, and most compliance frameworks. Authentication factors: something you know (passwords, PINs), something you have (private keys, hardware tokens), and something you are (fingerprints, facial recognition). True MFA requires factors from different categories.

content.title: Blue Team Lead, NEXUS Security
content.quote: A password and a security question are both things you know — that’s two steps, not two factors. A private key and a password? That’s real MFA. One factor gets stolen, you still have a wall between the attacker and your system.

content.part: Part A: Examine the current authentication policy

steps.text: Check what authentication methods your SSH server currently accepts:

whatThisDoes.concept: sshd -T dumps the SSH server’s effective configuration (all settings, including defaults). Piping through grep -i authenticationmethods filters for the line that controls which authentication methods are required.
whatThisDoes.output: If the output shows authenticationmethods any, the server accepts whatever works first — key or password. That’s single-factor.

content.part: Part B: Require both key and password

steps.text: Add the MFA directive to your SSH server configuration:

whatThisDoes.concept: This appends an MFA directive to the SSH server config. The syntax matters: a comma between methods means “AND” — the client must pass both. A space between methods means “OR” — the client can use either one. publickey,password = both required (MFA). publickey password = either one works (not MFA).

steps.text: Verify the line was added:
Restart the SSH service to apply the change:
Connect using your SSH config alias:

whatThisDoes.output: You should see two prompts in sequence: (1) Enter passphrase for key '...id_ed25519_lab': — unlocks your key file locally; (2) kali@localhost's password: — the server verifies your identity. Enter your key passphrase (nexus2026) at the first prompt, then your account password at the second. If both succeed, you’ll see your username from whoami.

content.title: Screenshot
content.text: Capture your successful MFA login showing both prompts and the whoami output. Save as 17-mfa-login.png — this is your last screenshot for Lab 2.2!

steps.text: Remove the MFA directive so later labs aren’t affected:
Restart SSH to apply changes:
Verify single-factor is restored:

content.title: Common Pitfalls
items: Comma vs. space: publickey,password = both required (MFA). publickey password = either one works (not MFA)
Forgot to restart SSH: Config changes don’t take effect until you restart the service
Key passphrase ≠ account password: Your passphrase (nexus2026) unlocks the key file locally. The account password is the separate factor the server verifies

content.title: What You Should Have
content.text: One screenshot showing a successful SSH login that required both your private key and your account password. That’s real two-factor authentication: something you have (the key file) plus something you know (the password).

content.title: Textbook Reference
content.text: Chapter 11 covers the three authentication factor categories and why MFA requires factors from different categories. The SSH MFA you just configured implements the same principle that HIPAA mandates for healthcare systems.

content.title: Dig Deeper
content.text: Research passwordless authentication. How do FIDO2/WebAuthn standards work, and why are they considered more secure than traditional password-based MFA?

content.title: That’s a Reference!
content.text: NIST SP 800-63B — Digital Identity Guidelines: Authentication and Lifecycle Management. Available at: https://pages.nist.gov/800-63-3/sp800-63b.html

items.label: SSH Key Theft
items.text: A stolen private key with no passphrase grants permanent access to every server that trusts it — no password to crack, no alert triggered.

items.label: Permission Reconnaissance
items.text: Misconfigured file permissions (644 on private keys, world-readable ~/.ssh) signal careless infrastructure and are a high-value target for lateral movement.

items.label: Single-Factor Exploitation
items.text: One compromised key with no MFA equals total access — attackers who obtain a key file from a misconfigured backup or shared directory own the system immediately.

items.label: Permission Hardening
items.text: Enforce key permissions as part of hardening checklists — audit scripts should flag any private key file not at 600 or ~/.ssh directory not at 700.

items.label: Passphrase Requirements
items.text: Require passphrases on all SSH keys used for privileged access; key-only login without a passphrase removes the 'something you know' factor entirely.

items.label: authorized_keys Inventory
items.text: Maintain authorized_keys inventory and audit for unknown entries — unrecognized keys in authorized_keys files are a persistence mechanism for attackers.

purpleTeam.connection: The permission demonstration in Checkpoint 2 shows exactly what attackers look for: private keys accessible to unprivileged processes. In Module 3 you will analyze network captures where the attacker's first post-compromise action is harvesting SSH keys from world-readable files — the same misconfiguration you fixed today.

finalChecklist.canvasQuiz: Take the Lab 2.2 Quiz in Canvas. Questions will ask about specific outputs you should have seen. Keep your VM running while taking the quiz.

needHelp.nextModule: After completing this lab, take the Lab 2.2 Quiz in Canvas, then proceed to Lab 2.3: Gulf Coast Certificate Remediation — the Week 3 engagement where you’ll apply your cryptography and authentication skills in a real client scenario.
needHelp.lateWorkPolicy: Late work is not accepted. Drop policies exist for emergencies.

permitted: Explain command syntax and tool functionality
prohibited: Complete hands-on exercises on your behalf
genAI.principle: AI is a tutor, not a ghostwriter.

whatsNext.text: After completing this lab and taking the Lab 2.2 Quiz in Canvas, proceed to Lab 2.3: Gulf Coast Certificate Remediation. You will SSH directly into a patient portal container using the skills from this lab, diagnose an expired TLS certificate, remediate it using the PKI workflow, and compile all three weeks into a complete Engagement Packet for the Gulf Coast Healthcare client.

customCallouts.title: Lab Environment Only
customCallouts.text: The SSH keys you generate and configure in this lab are for training purposes only. Never use lab-generated keys in production systems.
